title: Ramanujan Property and Edge Universality of Random Regular Graphs
speaker: Jiaoyang Huang
abstract:
Extremal eigenvalues of graphs are of particular interest in theoretical computer science and combinatorics. Specifically, the spectral gap—the difference between the largest and second-largest eigenvalues—measures the expansion properties of a graph. In this talk, I will focus on random d-regular graphs.
I will begin by providing background on the eigenvalues of random d-regular graphs and their connections to random matrix theory. In the second part of the talk, I will discuss our recent results on eigenvalue rigidity and edge universality for these graphs. Eigenvalue rigidity asserts that, with high probability, each eigenvalue concentrates around its classical location as predicted by the Kesten-McKay distribution. Edge universality states that the second-largest eigenvalue and the smallest eigenvalue of random d-regular graphs converge to the Tracy-Widom distribution from the Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble. Consequently, approximately 69% of d-regular graphs are Ramanujan graphs. This work is based on joint work with Theo McKenzie and Horng-Tzer Yau.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Random regular graph spectrum and Ramanujan threshold
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the uniform simple d-regular graph, its normalized adjacency matrix, the Kesten–McKay limiting law, and the Alon–Boppana/Ramanujan threshold |lambda|<=2 for optimal spectral expansion.

proof_stages.label: Tracy–Widom edge-law conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls GOE/GUE Tracy–Widom edge universality and numerical conjectures that the nontrivial extreme eigenvalues of random regular graphs have the GOE edge law after n^(2/3) scaling with no additional shift.

proof_stages.label: Eigenvalue rigidity, edge universality, and Ramanujan consequence
proof_stages.summary: The main eigenvalue theorems give near-optimal rigidity at Kesten–McKay quantiles and convergence of the two spectral edges to independent Tracy–Widom/Airy limits, which implies that asymptotically about 69% of fixed-degree random regular graphs are Ramanujan.

proof_stages.label: Edge eigenvectors as Gaussian waves
proof_stages.summary: The talk surveys delocalization and local Gaussian-wave limits for regular graph eigenvectors and states that the extreme eigenvectors converge locally to independent variance-one Gaussian waves.

proof_stages.label: Microscopic loop equations at the Airy edge
proof_stages.summary: Loop/Dyson–Schwinger equations for Stieltjes transforms of beta ensembles are introduced and then rescaled at the n^(-2/3) spectral edge to obtain universal equations satisfied by the Airy point process.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to loop equations for random regular graphs
proof_stages.summary: For the centered Stieltjes transform of the normalized adjacency matrix, the proof aims to verify finite-n loop equations with errors small enough for edge scaling, and a Gaussian-divisible interpolation is used to identify the limiting solution as the Airy/Tracy–Widom one.

proof_stages.label: Tree self-consistent equation for Green functions
proof_stages.summary: A Green-function/path-expansion heuristic, exploiting locally tree-like neighborhoods, leads to a cavity average q and a deterministic self-consistency relation q≈f(q) whose solution recovers the Kesten–McKay Stieltjes transform.

proof_stages.label: Generalized switching and local-resampling bootstrap
proof_stages.summary: Generalized simple switching produces an exchangeable local resampling, used as a discrete integration-by-parts mechanism to derive the self-consistency and loop-equation correction terms, then iterated to push the error below the n^(-2/3) edge scale.
